behaviors.description: Only files changed in the pull request are analyzed — unchanged files are not scanned
behaviors.justification: A Unit test of filter logic in isolation wouldn't catch if the action fails to read the GitHub changed-files API correctly or ignores the result. The failure boundary is the integration between GitHub's API (which defines the changed files) and the linter invocation. A mock of the GitHub API at Integration level can verify the linter only receives the correct file subset.
behaviors.test_description: Mock the GitHub API to return a specific list of changed files (e.g., 'src/main.ts', 'tests/util.ts'). Create additional files in the workspace not in that list (e.g., 'README.md', 'src/old.ts'). Run the action and verify via linter subprocess invocation logs that only the changed files were passed to the linter. This catches the failure where code scans all files instead of filtering by changed files.
behaviors.plan_consistent_note: Test requires mocking GitHub API and verifying linter subprocess input—Integration level. Cannot test at Unit without knowing where 'changed files' list originates.

behaviors.description: Findings are grouped by severity (error, warning, info) in the posted PR comment
behaviors.justification: While grouping logic could be Unit-tested in isolation, the behavior specifies findings must appear 'in the posted PR comment', requiring verification of the actual GitHub API call and formatted output. A pure Unit test of grouping-by-severity wouldn't catch formatting bugs in the comment or failures in the GitHub API integration. Integration-level mocking of GitHub reveals if the comment is posted with correct structure.
behaviors.test_description: Mock the GitHub API to capture the comment posted by the action. Create synthetic findings with mixed severities and run the linter. Verify the captured comment contains findings grouped under severity headers (e.g., '## Errors', '## Warnings', '## Infos') and findings appear in correct sections. This catches failures in grouping logic or comment formatting.
behaviors.plan_consistent_note: Test requires mocking GitHub API and inspecting posted comment structure—Integration level. A Unit test of grouping logic wouldn't verify the comment is posted correctly.

behaviors.description: A pull request that touches 200 files completes analysis within the 10-minute Actions timeout
behaviors.justification: Performance is environment-dependent. A local Integration test with real files might complete quickly in a developer machine but timeout on slower GitHub Actions runners due to runner hardware, network latency, and subprocess overhead. System-level testing in the actual GitHub Actions environment (or high-fidelity simulation of Actions runner specs) is necessary to catch real timeout failures that are specific to the CI environment.
behaviors.test_description: Create a temporary directory with 200 real files (varied sizes and complexity). Run the action in the GitHub Actions environment (or on an Actions-compatible runner) and measure end-to-end execution time from startup through comment posting. Verify completion within 10 minutes. This catches timeouts from real subprocess performance, GitHub API latency, and runner hardware.
behaviors.plan_consistent_note: Test requires 200 real files, real linter subprocess, real GitHub API calls, and execution on Actions infrastructure—System level. Local Integration test might be fast enough locally but fail in production Actions environment.

behaviors.description: When the linter process crashes on a malformed file, the remaining files are still analyzed and results posted
behaviors.justification: Error handling in the file-processing loop is an integration concern. A Unit test of error-handling code in isolation might pass even if the actual subprocess error isn't caught correctly or the pipeline doesn't recover. The failure boundary is how the file-processing loop responds to a subprocess crash. Integration-level testing with a mocked subprocess failure reveals whether error handling is correctly wired into the pipeline.
behaviors.test_description: Create a set of test files including one intentionally malformed (invalid syntax). Mock the linter subprocess to fail (non-zero exit) on the malformed file. Run the action and verify: (1) the malformed file is attempted, (2) the error is caught and logged, (3) remaining files are analyzed successfully, and (4) the final comment is posted with results from non-malformed files. This catches the failure where the action crashes entirely on one bad file.
behaviors.plan_consistent_note: Test requires integrating subprocess (mocked to fail) with the file-processing loop to verify resilience—Integration level. Unit test of error handling alone wouldn't verify the pipeline continues after a subprocess failure.

behaviors.description: Duplicate findings on the same line from overlapping rules are deduplicated in the output
behaviors.justification: Deduplication is a pure data transformation with no external dependencies (no filesystem, subprocess, network, or LLM involvement). A Unit test with synthetic findings is sufficient to catch failures in deduplication logic. The failure boundary is strictly the deduplication function's logic, not integration with GitHub, the linter, or the environment.
behaviors.test_description: Create synthetic findings where multiple linting rules flag the same issue on the same line (e.g., two rules both report 'error' at line 10, column 5, with identical rule IDs or descriptions). Pass these findings to the deduplication function and verify the output contains only one instance of that finding. This catches failures where duplicates aren't removed or where deduplication logic is broken.
behaviors.plan_consistent_note: Test requires only the deduplication function and synthetic data—Unit level. No subprocess, filesystem, or external APIs needed.
